intro: Account currency misconfiguration is a structural currency-state risk where ERP account settings, policy ownership, posting behavior, revaluation populations, or audit evidence diverge. The risk is not simply a wrong field; it is broken monetary custody.
doctrine_statement: Misconfigured account currency breaks the chain of custody before reporting can defend itself.
primary_section_body: A misconfigured account currency can alter which balances are maintained, revalued, reconciled, translated, reported, or audited.
The danger is amplified because many teams only discover misconfiguration after period-end exceptions, unexplained FX, failed reconciliations, migration errors, or audit evidence gaps.
This page defines risk patterns and control questions. It does not provide ERP implementation advice.

pillar_sections.title: Common account currency misconfiguration patterns
paragraphs: Default account creation assigns currency without policy review.
Migrated chart records preserve legacy behavior inconsistent with current entity policy.
Subledger currency fields map into accounts that carry incompatible custody assumptions.
Foreign monetary populations for revaluation are derived from stale configuration.
Reporting teams compensate through bridge spreadsheets instead of correcting custody.

steps.title: State 04 exposure
steps.body: The risk begins where account-level custody is assigned.

steps.title: ERP architecture
steps.body: Configuration must be evidenced and reconciled to policy.

steps.title: Diagnostic
steps.body: The diagnostic surfaces maturity gaps without collecting data.

pillar_sections.title: Evidence that should exist before the risk is closed
paragraphs: A resolved misconfiguration should leave a trace: issue ticket, affected account population, configuration extract, approval evidence, remediation journal support, revaluation rerun evidence, reconciliation sign-off, and a decision log explaining why the fix is complete.
Closing the issue without evidence simply transfers the risk into audit reality.

pillar_sections.title: Misconfiguration control questions
questions: Which accounts are affected and which currency state is wrong?
What policy or approval did the live configuration contradict?
Did the error affect revaluation, reconciliation, reporting, or consolidation?
Were historical periods restated, bridged, or disclosed internally?
Can the fix be reperformed from system extracts and approvals?
Was the root cause configuration, interface mapping, master data, migration, or governance?

graph_title: Misconfiguration is a custody failure, not a cosmetic defect.
graph_intro: The page connects ERP risk to AccountCcy's State 04, diagnostic surface, and audit evidence layer.

cards.label: ERP
cards.title: Wrong settings create wrong populations.
cards.body: Configuration errors can affect revaluation eligibility and reconciliation scope.

cards.label: Close
cards.title: Period-end exposes hidden drift.
cards.body: Misconfiguration often appears as unexplained FX or recurring reconciliation exceptions.

cards.label: Audit
cards.title: Evidence decides whether remediation is credible.
cards.body: A fix without traceability remains assurance debt.

control_title: Close misconfiguration with evidence, not assertion.
control_body: Require population analysis, root cause, configuration proof, journal impact, and reviewer sign-off.
risk_body: If misconfiguration is patched informally, future closes inherit uncertainty that is harder to isolate.

faq.question: Is account currency misconfiguration always material?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. Materiality requires professional judgment, but even immaterial errors may reveal weak governance.

faq.question: What is the first control step?
faq.answer: Identify affected account populations and prove live configuration against approved policy.
